result:
The hum begins at the edges of things. Not a sound you can hear, but a vibration you feel in the teeth, in the fillings. It’s the frequency of waiting. I am sitting at a kitchen table that is not mine, in a rented house by a lake that is too still. The light is the peculiar, thick gold of late afternoon in autumn, a light that doesn’t illuminate so much as gild, turning the dust on the windowsill into a trail of precious powder.

I think of all the tables I have sat at. The Formica one in my childhood home, sticky with the ghost of apricot jam. The rickety one in my first apartment, where a candle once burned a black scar into the pine. The vast, polished one in a corporate meeting room, where ideas went to die politely. Each table a stage for a different version of myself. The hungry child, the ambitious youth, the tired professional. Now, at this unknown table, I am just the observer. The one who notices the hum.

Out the window, the lake holds the sky like a cup. A leaf, a single maple leaf of furious scarlet, detaches itself. It doesn’t twirl or dance in the clichéd way of leaves in films. It sinks straight down, like a tiny, sinking ship, and lands perfectly flat on the water. It becomes a vessel. For a moment, I imagine it enormous, and myself miniature, boarding it. Setting sail from the shore of the known. Where would it take me? To the centre, to drown in the deep, clear cold? Or to the opposite shore, where the pines stand like silent, green-shawled monks?

The hum persists. It’s the sum of all the unattended thoughts, the unspoken words, the paths not taken. It’s the vibration of the dishwasher three houses down, the subsonic rumble of a truck on a distant highway, the slow tectonic grind of continents. It is the sound of time passing, not in ticks, but in a continuous, low-grade bleed.

I think about the archaeologist’s trick. They don’t just look for palaces and swords. They sift through middens—the ancient garbage pits. Fish bones, broken pottery, charcoal. The trivial defines a civilization more truly than its monuments. What, then, is my midden? The spent coffee grounds, the deleted emails, the single socks lost to the void behind the dryer. The careful, curated self I present online is the monument. The real self is in the discard pile: the irrational fears at 3 AM, the secret joy at a cancelled plan, the ungenerous thought swiftly swallowed.

A dragonfly, a shard of metallic blue, helicopters past the window. It is a machine of perfect purpose. It does not wonder about its purpose. It *is* purpose, flying. I envy it for a second, until I remember that its world is one of eat, mate, avoid being eaten. Its poetry is in its physics, not in its mind. It does not hear the hum. Or perhaps it is the source of it.

The gold light is deepening, clotting into amber. Shadows stretch, becoming long, attenuated versions of themselves. Soon, the lamps will have to be turned on. That moment of artificial light pushing back the gathering blue is always a small, domestic triumph. But not yet. I am resisting the lamp. I want to sit in the decay of the day, in this beautiful, melancholy fade.

We spend so much time building narratives. My life as a hero’s journey, a tragedy, a comedy, a saga of resilience. We force the chaotic, sparkling, messy data of existence into a storyline. “And then this happened, which led to that, and that’s why I am *here*.” But what if there is no storyline? What if it’s just this? A leaf sinking. A dragonfly hovering. A hum. A series of tables, one after another, until there are no more tables to sit at.

The leaf on the water has drifted. It has caught a micro-current and is beginning a slow, stately journey across the gilded surface. It is going somewhere, after all. I was wrong. It is not a ship for me. It is its own passenger.

I think of the people I have loved. Not in a sweeping, dramatic way, but in the specific, granular way. The exact curve of a smile that was only for me. The weight of a head on my shoulder in the passenger seat of a car at night. The particular timbre of a laugh from a friend I haven’t seen in years, which I can still conjure in the silent library of my memory. These are not monuments either. They are not the stories of the love. They are the midden treasures: the broken shards of shared jokes, the fish bones of old arguments, the charcoal of spent passions. They are more real to me now than the official histories.

The hum is changing. It is tuning itself to the coming night. It’s the prelude to the cricket symphony, the under-note for the loon’s lonely cry. I am part of the hum, too. The electrical storm in my skull, the soft pump of my heart, the rush of blood that is my own private ocean—it all adds to the frequency.

I will have to turn the lamp on soon. Make dinner. Re-enter the world of tasks and time. But for this suspended moment, I am nowhere and everywhere. I am at all the tables. I am the child, the youth, the professional, the observer. I am the leaf and the water and the light holding both.

This is the epiphany that comes not with a bang, but with a hum. There is no meaning to uncover, no grand plot. There is only attention. Paying attention is the prayer, the proof, the project. To see the leaf sink. To feel the light thicken. To hear the silence that isn’t silent at all, but teeming with vibration.

The first star pricks through the periwinkle hem of the sky. It is a pinprick of pure, cold light from a distant furnace. I take a breath, and the breath is part of the hum. I let it out.

I get up. My hand finds the lamp switch. *Click.* A small, yellow sun blooms in the room, pushing the majestic, dying gold of outside into mere darkness. The spell is broken, but it’s okay. The spell was never the point. The point was the noticing.

I go to make dinner. The water boils. The knife chops. These are new rhythms, joining the hum. And for now, for this evening in this borrowed house by this quiet lake, that is more than enough. It is everything.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
